terms.definition: The function L(θ) = f(x | θ) treating observed data x as fixed and the parameter θ as the variable; measures how well θ explains the data.
The natural logarithm of the likelihood, ℓ(θ) = log L(θ); for IID data it equals the sum of per-example log f(x_i | θ) and is what optimizers usually maximize.
Probability (or density) fixes θ and varies x with normalization over x; likelihood fixes x and varies θ without requiring normalization over θ.
The loss −ℓ(θ) or −log L(θ); minimizing average NLL on training data is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation.
For independent observations, L(θ) = ∏_i f(x_i | θ), the product of individual likelihood factors sharing the same parameter θ.
Inference about θ should depend on the experiment only through the likelihood function L(θ) for the observed data; contrasts with using unrelated aspects of the sampling plan.
